intro: None of this needs a new tool, a paid plan, or any technical skill — just a different habit. Every tip here works in ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or anything else with a text box.

groups.name: Never used one? Your first 20 minutes

tips.lead: Ask about something you know deeply.
tips.body: Your job, your hobby, your hometown. You'll see its strengths and flaws faster than any article can explain them.

tips.lead: Catch it being wrong — on purpose.
tips.body: Ask detailed questions about a niche topic you know well and hunt for small errors. Finding your first mistake is the moment AI stops being magic and starts being a tool.

tips.lead: Give it a real task, not a test.
tips.body: Rewrite an awkward email. Turn messy notes into a plan. Ask it to explain a confusing letter "like I'm not a lawyer." Real tasks show you what it's for.

tips.lead: Say who it's for and why.
tips.body: "Write a bio" vs. "a 2-sentence bio for my LinkedIn, friendly but professional." Same tool, wildly different result.
Don't describe your email — paste it in and ask for edits. AI edits real text far better than it guesses at imagined text.
One example of the output you want ("like this: …") locks in the pattern better than any description.

groups.name: While you're going back and forth

tips.body: "Make it shorter" or "less formal" builds on what's already good. Restarting throws away useful context.

tips.lead: Ask it to ask you questions first.
tips.body: For anything complex, try: "Ask me whatever you need to know before starting." It often catches gaps you didn't think to mention.

tips.lead: Say what to avoid, not just what you want.
tips.body: "Don't use corporate jargon" is often more useful than trying to describe the tone you're after.
Especially for names, taglines, or openings — comparing a few beats iterating on one.

tips.lead: If it seems wrong, say so.
tips.body: "Are you sure? Double-check that" genuinely improves accuracy — models often catch their own mistake when asked directly.
A vague "plan my trip" invites a generic answer. "First just list the cities, we'll do the rest after" gets a sharper one.

tips.lead: Hit a wall? Try a different model.
tips.body: No single AI is best at everything. The same question in a different chatbot can unlock a much better answer — see Today's tools, above.
No passwords, ID numbers, or confidential work documents. Treat a chatbot like a very smart stranger: helpful with anything you'd say out loud in public.
Chats aren't as private as they feel — depending on your settings, they can be stored and used to improve the AI. Every major chatbot has a privacy setting for this. Worth checking once.

tips.lead: Big decision? AI advises, you verify.
tips.body: For health, money, or legal questions, use AI to understand your situation and prepare better questions — then confirm with a professional or an official source before acting.
Most chatbots let you set standing preferences (a **[system prompt](#term-system-prompt)** or "custom instructions") so you don't re-explain your tone every time.
Coding assistants take these same ideas further, letting AI act across many steps on its own — see **[AI Agent](#term-ai-agent)** and **[Vibe coding](#term-vibe-coding)** in Key terms, below.
